current_fact.fact_text: On 12 April 1204, Crusader armies of the Fourth Crusade breached the walls of Constantinople and entered the fabled Byzantine capital. Instead of reaching the Holy Land, they spent days looting one of the richest cities on Earth, hauling away relics, statues, and even bronze horses. The sack permanently fractured Eastern and Western Christendom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

current_fact.match_reason: A crusader army diverted into Byzantine politics pairs with an episode about another layered Mediterranean crossroads: Spain and the hybrid history behind flamenco.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1159, Byzantine emperor Manuel I Komnenos entered Antioch in triumph after securing the submission of its prince, Raynald of Châtillon. The spectacle signaled Byzantine resurgence, with Manuel parading through a Crusader city that had long slipped from Constantinople’s control. It was as much theater as politics, crafted to project imperial glory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: Manuel’s pageantry in a contested Eastern city resonates with an episode traveling between Mediterranean cultures and mountain landscapes, blending power, ritual, and scenery.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 627, King Edwin of Northumbria was baptized as a Christian by Paulinus, Bishop of York, in what is now northern England. His conversion helped accelerate the spread of Christianity across Anglo-Saxon kingdoms, turning riversides and wooden halls into new ritual spaces. Political alliance and spiritual shift were intertwined in a single ceremony.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: A spiritual and cultural shift in early northern Britain fits an episode about quiet transitions and modern music under nearby Scottish skies.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 467, Anthemius was proclaimed Western Roman emperor, one of the last rulers to hold that fading title. A capable general brought west from Constantinople, he tried to hold together a crumbling empire beset by internal rivalries and external invasions. His short reign shows how late Roman emperors were often more stopgap solutions than stable rulers.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The beginning of Anthemius’ brief, pivotal reign matches Sedna’s own ‘where it all began’ first broadcast—both key openings before turbulent chapters.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1877, the United Kingdom formally annexed the Transvaal, a Boer-ruled republic in southern Africa, citing the region’s financial troubles and frontier conflicts. The move ignited tensions that would later feed into the Anglo-Boer Wars. On maps, a line changed color; on the ground, identities, land, and language collided.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A colonial annexation in southern Africa is placed alongside an episode celebrating African musical self-expression and postcolonial cultural power.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1970, the Soviet nuclear submarine K-8 sank in the Bay of Biscay after fighting an onboard fire for four days, taking four nuclear torpedoes and most of its crew to the seafloor. The accident highlighted how little was known about the long-term behavior of sunken nuclear weapons in the ocean. The wreck still lies under thousands of meters of water, a quiet radioactive relic of the Cold War.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A submarine lost to the deep ocean pairs with an episode about immersion and going below the surface—shifting from military secrecy to contemplative depth.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1963, the Soviet nuclear-powered submarine K-33 collided with the Finnish merchant ship M/S Finnclipper in the Danish Straits. Though serious, the incident remained shrouded in secrecy for decades, typical of Cold War maritime near-disasters. Ships carrying ordinary cargo and vessels carrying reactors passed within meters of each other in some of the world’s busiest waterways.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A secretive submarine collision in northern waters contrasts with an open, awe-filled journey among whales in nearby Arctic seas.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1999, during NATO’s bombing of Yugoslavia, an American F-15E Strike Eagle accidentally hit a passenger train at Grdelica Gorge while attacking a railway bridge. The pilot released a second bomb even after seeing the train, relying on the weapon’s programmed path, and between 20 and 60 civilians were killed. The incident became a grim example of how modern precision weapons can still produce tragic errors.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A deadly wartime mistake intersects with an episode about political protest music, anger at systemic violence, and questioning the machinery of power.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1990, Widerøe Flight 839, a small de Havilland Twin Otter, crashed shortly after takeoff from Værøy Airport in Norway amid extreme wind conditions. All five people on board were killed, and the accident led to the closure of the exposed island airfield. The crash underscored how microclimates and mountain winds can turn short flights into serious engineering puzzles.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A tragic flight in harsh winds is paired with an episode inviting slower, more grounded movement, as if to counter the rush that weather sometimes punishes.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 2009, Zimbabwe officially abandoned the Zimbabwean dollar after hyperinflation rendered it nearly useless, with prices doubling in hours and banknotes printed in trillion-dollar denominations. The country shifted to foreign currencies like the US dollar and South African rand, effectively erasing its own money to stabilize daily life. It remains one of the most extreme monetary collapses in modern history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: An economy learning from catastrophic hyperinflation and starting again resonates with an episode about failure, adaptation, and long-term learning.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1990—the same day his dinosaur show opened—sculptor Jim Gary also became a symbol of how art can reshape our relationship to nature and technology. His brightly colored ‘scrap metal saurians’ invited children to touch, climb, and walk among creatures assembled from discarded machine parts. It was paleontology rewritten in steel and chrome, playful rather than dusty.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

queue.match_reason: Reimagining dinosaurs from scrap celebrates unconventional beauty and creativity, matching an episode about embracing unique, unexpected selves.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1961, the world also quietly marked a cultural milestone: the word “cosmonaut” entered everyday language alongside “astronaut”. Gagarin’s flight sparked not just a technical race, but a race of stories, posters, songs and films about life beyond Earth. Space travel stopped being only science fiction and became a reachable dream—for some, a new kind of myth.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: The birth of the cosmonaut myth fits an evening mix that starts cosmic (“Solar Wind”) and then travels across Earth’s cultures like an orbit in sound.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1961, as Vostok 1 circled the globe, people on the ground strained to hear Gagarin’s voice on crackling radios. Some Soviet citizens first learned of spaceflight not through official bulletins but via whispered rumors and intercepted broadcasts. For a few hours, the planet itself became a kind of shared listening party, tuned to a single human heartbeat in orbit.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: A world listening intently to a fragile radio voice from orbit resonates with an episode about deliberate, focused listening in an age of infinite noise.

queue.fact_text: On 12 April 1961, the Space Race abruptly tilted: a 27-year-old test pilot from a village near Smolensk beat the United States into orbit. Gagarin’s simple exclamation—“Poyekhali!” (“Let’s go!”)—captured a moment where fear, pride, and wonder were compressed into a single launch. For both superpowers, space suddenly became not just a frontier, but a stage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A historic leap into the ‘cosmic’ age aligns with an episode built around cosmic disco and the futuristic glow of machines and rhythm.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1961, Yuri Gagarin became the first human to leave Earth, spending just 108 minutes in orbit aboard Vostok 1. His single loop around the planet instantly turned him into a global icon and proved that human spaceflight was possible. The mission was fully automated in case Gagarin lost consciousness under space conditions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal ‘space shift’ for humanity pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as possibility and momentum.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1981, Space Shuttle Columbia lifted off on STS-1, the first orbital flight of NASA’s partially reusable shuttle. Unusually for a maiden voyage, it carried two astronauts and a full flight profile, testing whether a winged spacecraft could survive both launch and runway landing. The shuttle’s black thermal tiles became an icon of a new era of space transportation.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A bold new ‘destination’ into reusable spaceflight is matched with a voyage-themed episode that wanders into new territories.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1985, Space Shuttle Discovery launched on STS-51-D, a mission that was supposed to be a straightforward deployment of two communications satellites. One satellite failed to boost itself into proper orbit, forcing astronauts to improvise an unscripted spacewalk using the shuttle’s robotic arm in an early example of orbital ‘repair work’. It hinted at how dependent Earth was becoming on quiet machines circling overhead.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A mission about launching communications satellites fits an episode reflecting on our hyper-connected lives and dependence on invisible networks.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1934, observers atop Mount Washington in New Hampshire recorded a surface wind gust of 231 mph (372 km/h), then the strongest reliably measured on Earth. The tiny weather station was rocked by hurricane-force winds and ice, showing just how extreme conditions can be on a relatively modest mountain. For decades, this lonely summit was known as the ‘Home of the World’s Worst Weather’.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: Record-shattering winds are a natural ‘electric shock’, matching the episode’s raw, high-voltage rock energy.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1990, the exhibition “Twentieth Century Dinosaurs” by sculptor Jim Gary opened at the Smithsonian’s National Museum of Natural History. Gary built life-sized dinosaurs entirely from recycled car parts, turning scrap metal into colorful prehistoric creatures. He remains the only sculptor ever invited to present a solo exhibition at that museum.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: Turning discarded car parts into dinosaurs echoes reggaeton’s transformation of marginalized, ‘scrap’ sounds into a dominant cultural force.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1862, a small band of Union raiders stole a locomotive named The General in Georgia, sparking the “Great Locomotive Chase”. Pursued almost the entire way by Confederate crews on foot and by train, they tried to sabotage tracks and telegraph lines behind them. The wild pursuit became one of the most cinematic episodes of the American Civil War.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A frantic train heist and chase match an ‘on the go’ episode built for motion, travel, and forward momentum.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 2014, the Great Fire of Valparaíso swept through the hills above the Chilean port city, killing 16 people and destroying over 2,000 homes. The blaze moved so fast through the maze of colorful hillside houses that entire neighborhoods vanished overnight. In the aftermath, residents painted murals and rebuilt, turning ruins into a new layer of the city’s visual story.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating urban fire and slow rebuilding journey fit an episode about weathering storms and holding onto long-term hope.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1945, the U.S. Ninth Army crossed the Elbe River near Magdeburg in the final push into Nazi Germany. Their advance brought Allied forces within about 80 kilometers of Berlin, yet political decisions would soon halt them short of the German capital. The crossing symbolized how close the war in Europe was to its brutal end.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: A tense river crossing near the end of a war pairs with a moody, cinematic trip-hop journey that feels like moving through fog toward an uncertain horizon.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1782, Admiral George Rodney’s Royal Navy fleet defeated the French under the Comte de Grasse at the Battle of the Saintes in the Caribbean. Using a daring maneuver to break the enemy line, Rodney captured de Grasse and shattered French naval hopes in the West Indies. The battle’s swirling clouds of cannon smoke and shattered masts turned the sea into a chaotic wooden maze.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A historic Caribbean naval battle naturally connects to an ocean-sailing themed episode, shifting from warships to peaceful voyages over the waves.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1796, a young Napoleon Bonaparte won his first victory as an army commander at the Battle of Montenotte in northern Italy. By splitting the Austrian and Piedmontese forces, he began a rapid campaign that would push Piedmont toward surrender. The battle marked the arrival of a new kind of military celebrity, one who understood both maneuver and myth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

published.match_reason: Napoleon’s first big win is the start of his own ‘star treatment’, mirroring an episode about the rise and transformation of a cultural star.

published.fact_text: On 12 April 1204, Crusader armies of the Fourth Crusade breached the walls of Constantinople and entered the fabled Byzantine capital. Instead of reaching the Holy Land, they spent days looting one of the richest cities on Earth, hauling away relics, statues, and even bronze horses. The sack permanently fractured Eastern and Western Christendom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A crusader army diverted into Byzantine politics pairs with an episode about another layered Mediterranean crossroads: Spain and the hybrid history behind flamenco.
